participants.extracted: Adult cigarette smokers and a never-smoker comparison group were recruited at five U.S. clinical research units. The randomized smoker sample included 125 participants at baseline (intent-to-treat): 44 assigned to tobacco-heating cigarettes, 43 to snus, and 44 to ultra-low machine-yield tobacco-burning cigarettes. Thirty-two never smokers were included for baseline comparisons. For change-from-baseline analyses in the per-protocol sample, 33 participants remained in the tobacco-heating cigarette group, 20 in the snus group, and 35 in the ultra-low machine-yield tobacco-burning cigarette group. Participants were adult, predominantly White, and the per-protocol sample was predominantly male in the snus group.

interventions.extracted: Adult cigarette smokers were randomized to switch for 24 weeks to one of three alternate tobacco products: a tobacco-heating cigarette (Eclipse, non-menthol or menthol depending on preference), snus (Camel SNUS, with subject choice of Frost, Spice, or Mellow), or an ultra-low machine-yield tobacco-burning cigarette (5 mg Cambridge Filter Method “tar”; Camel or Salem, non-menthol or menthol depending on preference).

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure tobacco exposure biomarkers in urine and blood, including nicotine uptake assessed by total nicotine equivalents (NICEq, 24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; NNK exposure assessed by total NNAL (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; aromatic amine exposure assessed by urinary 3-aminobiphenyl, 4-aminobiphenyl, 2-aminonaphthalene, and o-toluidine (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon exposure assessed by urinary 1-naphthol, 2-naphthol, 2-hydroxyfluorene, 1-/9-hydroxyphenanthrene, 2-/3-hydroxyphenanthrene, and 1-hydroxypyrene (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; acrylamide exposure assessed by urinary AAMA and GAMA (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; 1,3-butadiene exposure assessed by urinary MHBMA and DHBMA (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; crotonaldehyde exposure assessed by urinary HMPMA (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; acrolein exposure assessed by urinary HPMA (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; benzene exposure assessed by urinary SPMA (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; urine mutagenicity assessed by Ames assay revertants in TA98 and YG1024 strains (24-hour urine) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; serum cotinine (blood) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; carboxyhemoglobin/COHb percent saturation (blood) at weeks 0, 12, and 24; and 4-aminobiphenyl hemoglobin adducts (4-ABP-Hb, blood) at weeks 0, 12, and 24. The study also planned mouth-level exposure outcomes in the ultra-low machine yield cigarette group, including char line length (mm), MLE nicotine per cigarette (mg/cigarette) and per day (mg/day), and MLE tar per cigarette (mg/cigarette) and per day (mg/day), assessed prior to confinement and within confinement at weeks 0, 12, and 24, plus comparisons of nicotine yield-in-use versus nicotine uptake over the same time points.
